body_txt: A character who identifies as a devout Christian—most often Evangelical or otherwise Protestant (and in rare cases Orthodoxy )—but displays clear bias or hostility toward the Catholic Church and its followers. This usually stems from theological differences, with the character viewing Catholic beliefs and practices as unbiblical, idolatrous, or spiritually misguided. Depending on the story, this attitude may be treated as justified, portrayed as ignorance or prejudice, or challenged through Character Development . It should be noted that the term "Protestant" is actually derived from the 16th century movement to reform the Catholic Church of the Holy Roman Empire due to suspected corruption and abuse from within, leading to protests against the Church from those joining the movement. As a result, one doesn't have to look very far in history to find examples of Protestants taking sides against Catholics (and vice versa) in unpleasant conflicts all the way up to full-on national wars. The Thirty Years' War and the Tudor Era in particular provide plenty of fodder for religious historical drama. Generally, Protestant thinkers tended to see the Catholic tradition as being mired in excessive ritual and superstitious, headed by corrupt authorities , and filled with traditions that they perceived as being either pagan influences or secretly being paganism outright , particularly sainthood and various holiday rituals. The modern form of this dynamic has deep roots in the United States. Anti-Catholic sentiment dates back to early Protestant settlers, who brought longstanding European religious conflicts with them and often excluded Catholics from full civic participation. The hostility peaked in the 19th century with large waves of Catholic immigration, when Catholic newcomers such as the Irish, Italians, Germans, and Poles were frequently seen as too foreign to assimilate, politically suspect, and beholden to the Pope, leading to widespread discrimination and even violence. In fact, whenever a character uses the term "Papist" to refer to Catholics, it's an almost sure sign that they're this sort of character. The old English version was "popish" - a truly terrible adjective. While overt anti-Catholicism has declined significantly in modern America, especially as Catholics became more socially and politically integrated, elements of this attitude persist in some religious circles. Certain Evangelical and fundamentalist groups continue to reject Catholicism as doctrinally invalid or “not truly Christian,” though this often coexists with areas of cooperation on shared social or political issues. In contemporary times, a shared struggle against progressive secularism has often placed Conservative Catholics and Protestants on the same side, though a degree of animosity may persist . This trope often overlaps with No True Scotsman when the character doesn't consider Catholics truly "Christian". It also often overlaps with Holier than Thou or With Us or Against Us . If either side overlaps with Church Militant , look out. By rule, the trope averts Christianity Is Catholic . A lot of works set during The Troubles feature this trope. Contrast Ambivalent Anglican , when a character belongs to the Church of England but has no particular interest in doctrinal specifics. Also contrast Ambiguously Christian , when a character is apparently Christian but any details about their denomination are avoided. Compare and contrast Culturally Religious , since one’s cultural upbringing may or may not entrench and reinforce prejudices. Despite being Truth in Television , No Real Life Examples, Please! This trope's presence in real life is too gossipy and prejudicial by nature to be worth discussing non-fictional examples of here.
